title: Tactics: Desperado
summary: Learn to sacrifice by moving as if you're already lost - but winning.
description: A desperado is when a piece that seems doomed captures another piece, knowing it will be captured, but the capture results in a better outcome - often checkmate or winning material. This is a powerful tactical concept used by GMs.
objectives: Recognize positions where a desperado is possible
Understand when a piece is 'doomed' anyway
Learn to calculate the exchange favorably
Master the timing of desperado moves
plans: Ask: 'If my piece is going to die anyway, can it capture something first?'
Look for captures where the attacker also gets checkmate or a winning position
Often the desperado piece captures something valuable before being captured
commonMistakes: Don't play desperado if your piece could escape
Calculate the full sequence - sometimes the opponent can defend
The piece should be doomed, not just exposed
completionTitle: Making the most of doomed pieces
completionSummary: You've learned to use the desperado concept to turn losing positions around.

steps.title: Control the center
steps.hint: Push the king pawn two squares.

steps.title: Recapture
steps.hint: Capture on d4 with the c-pawn.

steps.title: Black develops
steps.instruction: Black moves the bishop to b6.

steps.title: Push e5
steps.instruction: Play e5! This attacks the knight on f6.

steps.title: Black defends
steps.instruction: Black pushes d5, attacking the bishop.
steps.coaching: The pawn attacks the bishop on c4. But look at the knight on f6...

steps.title: The Desperado!
steps.instruction: Capture on f6 with the pawn! Even though it will be captured, this gives us a strong attack!
steps.coaching: This is a desperado! The pawn is going to be captured anyway, but we get a strong attack and open lines against the king.
steps.hint: Capture the knight on f6 with the pawn!

steps.title: Black captures
steps.coaching: Black captures, but now look at the position - we have open lines against Black's king!
